title: Pitești

infobox.value: (Romanian pronunciation: [[piˈteʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q7913_(ron)-KlaudiuMihaila-Pite%C8%99ti.wav "File:LL-Q7913 (ron)-KlaudiuMihaila-Pitești.wav")) is a city in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), located on the river [Argeș](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_(river) "Argeș (river)"). The capital and largest city of [Argeș County](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County "Argeș County"), it is an important commercial and industrial center, as well as the home of two universities. Pitești is situated in the historical region of [Muntenia](/wiki/Muntenia "Muntenia"). It lies on the [A1 freeway](/wiki/A1_freeway_(Romania) "A1 freeway (Romania)"), connecting the city directly to the national capital [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), being an important railway junction, with a [classification yard](/wiki/Classification_yard "Classification yard") in nearby [Bălilești](/wiki/B%C4%83lile%C8%99ti "Bălilești"). The city houses the [Arpechim oil refinery](/wiki/Arpechim_Refinery "Arpechim Refinery"), and is a marketing center for the [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Romania "Automotive industry in Romania"), in particular, [Automobile Dacia](/wiki/Automobile_Dacia "Automobile Dacia").

**Pitești** (Romanian pronunciation: [[piˈteʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q7913_(ron)-KlaudiuMihaila-Pite%C8%99ti.wav "File:LL-Q7913 (ron)-KlaudiuMihaila-Pitești.wav")) is a city in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), located on the river [Argeș](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_(river) "Argeș (river)"). The capital and largest city of [Argeș County](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County "Argeș County"), it is an important commercial and industrial center, as well as the home of two universities. Pitești is situated in the historical region of [Muntenia](/wiki/Muntenia "Muntenia"). It lies on the [A1 freeway](/wiki/A1_freeway_(Romania) "A1 freeway (Romania)"), connecting the city directly to the national capital [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), being an important railway junction, with a [classification yard](/wiki/Classification_yard "Classification yard") in nearby [Bălilești](/wiki/B%C4%83lile%C8%99ti "Bălilești"). The city houses the [Arpechim oil refinery](/wiki/Arpechim_Refinery "Arpechim Refinery"), and is a marketing center for the [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Romania "Automotive industry in Romania"), in particular, [Automobile Dacia](/wiki/Automobile_Dacia "Automobile Dacia").

Inhabited since [prehistoric times](/wiki/Prehistory_of_the_Balkans "Prehistory of the Balkans") but first mentioned in the 14th century, it developed as a trading town in northern [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia"), serving as an informal residence for various [Wallachian princes](/wiki/List_of_princes_of_Wallachia "List of princes of Wallachia") until the 18th century. From the 19th century and until the interwar period, it was an important political center for the [National Liberal Party](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Romania) "National Liberal Party (Romania)") and the main residence of the [Brătianu family](/wiki/Br%C4%83tianu_family "Brătianu family") of politicians. During the early stages of the [communist regime](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania"), it was one of the main sites of political repression, with the [Pitești Prison](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti_Prison "Pitești Prison") becoming home to an experiment in [brainwashing](/wiki/Brainwashing "Brainwashing") techniques.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The earliest traces of human settlements in this area relate to the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic").[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3) Coins minted by the [Dacians](/wiki/Dacians "Dacians") during the 3rd century BC, copying the design of [Thracian](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") *[tetradrachmon](/wiki/Ancient_drachma "Ancient drachma")* issued by [Lysimachus](/wiki/Lysimachus "Lysimachus"), have been discovered here.[[4]](#cite_note-4) A small [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") *[castrum](/wiki/Castrum "Castrum")* was built sometime in the 3rd century [AD](/wiki/AD "AD") in the vicinity of present-day Pitești (part of a protection system for [Roman Dacia](/wiki/Roman_Dacia "Roman Dacia") and [Moesia](/wiki/Moesia "Moesia")).[[5]](#cite_note-5) During the [Age of Migrations](/wiki/Age_of_Migrations "Age of Migrations"), the Pitești area was, according to historian [Constantin C. Giurescu](/wiki/Constantin_C._Giurescu "Constantin C. Giurescu"), the site of trading between [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") and [Slavs](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples"), which, in his opinion, was the origin of *Târgul din Deal* ("The Market on the Hill"), a separate locality.[[6]](#cite_note-Giurescu,_p.47-6)

The first recorded mention of Pitești itself was on May 20, 1386, when [Wallachian Prince](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Wallachia "List of rulers of Wallachia") [Mircea I](/wiki/Mircea_I_of_Wallachia "Mircea I of Wallachia") granted a [gristmill](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill") in the area to [Cozia Monastery](/wiki/Cozia_Monastery "Cozia Monastery").[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3)[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) Pitești was subsequently one of the temporary residences of Wallachian princes.[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3) Due to its positioning on the junction of major European routes (and its proximity to the [Saxon](/wiki/Transylvanian_Saxons "Transylvanian Saxons") markets in [Hermannstadt](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu"), [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania")), the city originally developed as an important commercial center.[[8]](#cite_note-8) By the late 14th century, it became home to a sizable [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians_in_Romania "Armenians in Romania") community.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

At the time, the locality only extended on the left bank of the Argeș, and gradually expanded over the river, reaching the hill slopes to the west[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3) (in the 19th century, it completely absorbed *Târgul din Deal*).[[6]](#cite_note-Giurescu,_p.47-6) While Pitești was commonly designated as a high-ranking town, a village of Pitești was still mentioned as late as 1528, which led some historians to conclude that the village and urban area coexisted within the same boundaries.[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7)

### Early Modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Modern period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Pitesti%2C_Curtea_de_Arges%2C_Campulung_%28Cantacuzino_map%29.PNG/250px-Pitesti%2C_Curtea_de_Arges%2C_Campulung_%28Cantacuzino_map%29.PNG)](/wiki/File:Pitesti,_Curtea_de_Arges,_Campulung_(Cantacuzino_map).PNG)

Pitești, [Câmpulung](/wiki/C%C3%A2mpulung "Câmpulung") and [Curtea de Argeș](/wiki/Curtea_de_Arge%C8%99 "Curtea de Argeș") on the [Constantin Cantacuzino](/wiki/Constantin_Cantacuzino_(stolnic) "Constantin Cantacuzino (stolnic)") map of 1718

Although princely quarters have not been uncovered, among the rulers to issue documents from Pitești were [Basarab Țepeluș cel Tânăr](/wiki/Basarab_%C8%9Aepelu%C8%99_cel_T%C3%A2n%C4%83r "Basarab Țepeluș cel Tânăr") (1477–1481), [Neagoe Basarab](/wiki/Neagoe_Basarab "Neagoe Basarab") (1512–1521), [Vlad Înecatul](/wiki/Vlad_%C3%8Enecatul "Vlad Înecatul") (1530–1532), [Vlad Vintilă de la Slatina](/wiki/Vlad_Vintil%C4%83_de_la_Slatina "Vlad Vintilă de la Slatina") (1532–1535), [Michael the Brave](/wiki/Michael_the_Brave "Michael the Brave") (1593–1601), [Simion Movilă](/wiki/Simion_Movil%C4%83 "Simion Movilă") (1601–1602), [Matei Basarab](/wiki/Matei_Basarab "Matei Basarab") (1632–1654) and [Constantin Șerban](/wiki/Constantin_%C8%98erban "Constantin Șerban") (1654–1658).[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) In addition, [Constantin Brâncoveanu](/wiki/Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncoveanu "Constantin Brâncoveanu") (1688–1714), who owned large sections of vineyard in the area, is reported to have spent several seasons in the town.[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7)

Under Vlad Vintilă, who allied himself with the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") against his [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") overlords, [Aloisio Gritti](/wiki/Aloisio_Gritti "Aloisio Gritti") (governor of [Ottoman Hungary](/wiki/Ottoman_Hungary "Ottoman Hungary")) and his Wallachian [boyar](/wiki/Boyar "Boyar") partisans camped in the Pitești neighborhood of Războieni, where they were attacked and defeated by the prince.[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) In 1600–1601, troops of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), led by [Jan Zamoyski](/wiki/Jan_Zamoyski "Jan Zamoyski"), were stationed in Pitești during their expedition against Michael the Brave (*see [Moldavian Magnate Wars](/wiki/Moldavian_Magnate_Wars "Moldavian Magnate Wars")*).[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7)[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10) Around that time, fighting occurred in and around the town as the new prince [Radu Șerban](/wiki/Radu_%C8%98erban "Radu Șerban") clashed with the Ottomans and their [Crimean Khanate](/wiki/Crimean_Khanate "Crimean Khanate") allies.[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10)

Constantin Șerban financed the building of the [Orthodox](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church") Saint George Church, completed in 1656.[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3)[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) His building program also included a (since-lost) palace and adjacent gardens.[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) Around that time, the city hosted travelers of renown, such as Arab chronicler [Paul of Aleppo](/wiki/Paul_of_Aleppo "Paul of Aleppo") and Swedish politician [Claes Rålamb](/wiki/Claes_R%C3%A5lamb_(1622%E2%80%931698) "Claes Rålamb (1622–1698)").[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10) It was during Brâncoveanu's rule that the city was home to *[Stolnic](/wiki/Stolnic "Stolnic")* [Constantin Cantacuzino](/wiki/Constantin_Cantacuzino_(stolnic) "Constantin Cantacuzino (stolnic)"), who was exchanging letters with English statesman [William, Baron Paget](/wiki/William_Paget,_6th_Baron_Paget "William Paget, 6th Baron Paget").[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) A tower and other princely houses, built by Brâncoveanu outside the town, gradually deteriorated over the 18th and 19th centuries (the last standing structure was lost in the 20th century).[[7]](#cite_note-gcalin-7) In 1689, [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") troops led by [Louis William of Baden](/wiki/Louis_William,_Margrave_of_Baden-Baden "Louis William, Margrave of Baden-Baden") occupied the city as part of the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") (they were repelled later that year).[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10)

### 18th and early 19th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 18th and early 19th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Illustration_from_Views_in_the_Ottoman_Dominions_by_Luigi_Mayer%2C_digitally_enhanced_by_rawpixel-com_24.jpg/250px-Illustration_from_Views_in_the_Ottoman_Dominions_by_Luigi_Mayer%2C_digitally_enhanced_by_rawpixel-com_24.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illustration_from_Views_in_the_Ottoman_Dominions_by_Luigi_Mayer,_digitally_enhanced_by_rawpixel-com_24.jpg)

Pitești in 1793, etching by [Luigi Mayer](/wiki/Luigi_Mayer "Luigi Mayer")

In November 1714, as a direct result of Swedish defeats in the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War") against [Imperial Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia"), [Swedish King](/wiki/List_of_Swedish_monarchs "List of Swedish monarchs") [Charles XII](/wiki/Charles_XII_of_Sweden "Charles XII of Sweden") unsuccessfully sought an alliance with [Sultan](/wiki/Ottoman_Dynasty "Ottoman Dynasty") [Ahmed III](/wiki/Ahmed_III "Ahmed III"); on his way back from [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), Charles and [Axel Sparre](/wiki/Axel_Sparre "Axel Sparre") passed through Pitești, and, after a three-week stay, made their way to [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania") through Habsburg-ruled regions.[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10) During the [Austro-Turkish War of 1716–1718](/wiki/Austro-Turkish_War_of_1716-18 "Austro-Turkish War of 1716-18"), Habsburg troops attacked and captured the town; Pitești was again the scene of battles during the [Austro-Turkish War of 1737–1739](/wiki/Austro-Turkish_War,_1737%E2%80%931739 "Austro-Turkish War, 1737–1739").[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10)

In 1780, [Tuscan](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany") numismatist [Domenico Sestini](/w/index.php?title=Domenico_Sestini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Domenico Sestini (page does not exist)") passed through the Argeș region, and described the town as having 250 houses and 7 churches.[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10) In 1804, the citizens requested to have an upper school opened (to offer lectures in Greek, the educational language of the time); their request was denied by Prince [Constantine Ypsilantis](/wiki/Constantine_Ypsilantis "Constantine Ypsilantis").[[11]](#cite_note-11) During the 1790s, Pitești was visited by [Luigi Mayer](/wiki/Luigi_Mayer "Luigi Mayer"), a German pupil of [Giovanni Battista Piranesi](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Piranesi "Giovanni Battista Piranesi"), who left etchings of the region (including the very first one of Pitești);[[10]](#cite_note-mpaduraru-10) they were published in London in 1810, with text by a T. Bowyer, whose caption for Pitești read "nothing more wild or romantic can be conceived".[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The town was an important location for events relating to the last stage of the [Wallachian uprising of 1821](/wiki/Wallachian_uprising_of_1821 "Wallachian uprising of 1821") and the first stages of the [Greek War of Independence](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence"): it was here that, in late spring 1821, the Wallachian rebel leader [Tudor Vladimirescu](/wiki/Tudor_Vladimirescu "Tudor Vladimirescu") settled after retreating from [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). His departure raised suspicion from his [Eterist](/wiki/Filiki_Eteria "Filiki Eteria") allies that he was planning to abandon the common cause. Vladimirescu was captured in the nearby locality of [Băilești](/wiki/B%C4%83ile%C8%99ti "Băilești") and executed soon after, on orders from [Alexander Ypsilantis](/wiki/Alexander_Ypsilantis_(1792%E2%80%931828) "Alexander Ypsilantis (1792–1828)").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Late 19th and early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Late 19th and early 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Biserica_Sf._Nicolae_Pitesti.jpg/250px-Biserica_Sf._Nicolae_Pitesti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biserica_Sf._Nicolae_Pitesti.jpg)

Postcard of Saint Nicholas Church, early 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Interbelic_Pitesti_CoA.png/250px-Interbelic_Pitesti_CoA.png)](/wiki/File:Interbelic_Pitesti_CoA.png)

Coat of arms of Pitești during the interwar period

The city was developed further after the [1859 unification](/wiki/United_Principalities "United Principalities") of the [Danubian Principalities](/wiki/Danubian_Principalities "Danubian Principalities") and the 1881 creation of the [Romanian Kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"). Around that time, and down to the late interwar, the city became a [National Liberal](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Romania) "National Liberal Party (Romania)") center, largely due to the [Brătianu family](/wiki/Br%C4%83tianu_family "Brătianu family") of politicians residing in nearby [Ștefănești](/wiki/%C8%98tef%C4%83ne%C8%99ti,_Arge%C8%99 "Ștefănești, Argeș").[[14]](#cite_note-mcraciun-14) Their manor, *[Florica](/wiki/Vila_Florica "Vila Florica")*, housed most major reunions of the National Liberal leaders.[[14]](#cite_note-mcraciun-14) For a short period in 1882, Pitești was home to dramatist [Ion Luca Caragiale](/wiki/Ion_Luca_Caragiale "Ion Luca Caragiale"), leading some to propose that it was the unnamed National Liberal-dominated city depicted in Caragiale's famous play *[O scrisoare pierdută](/wiki/O_scrisoare_pierdut%C4%83 "O scrisoare pierdută")*.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

By 1872, a [national railway](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române") connection with the capital Bucharest and [Târgoviște](/wiki/T%C3%A2rgovi%C8%99te "Târgoviște") was built, at the same time as one linking Bucharest with [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești") through [Chitila](/wiki/Chitila "Chitila").[[16]](#cite_note-Giurescu,_p.155-16) Overseen by the German financier [Bethel Henry Strousberg](/wiki/Bethel_Henry_Strousberg "Bethel Henry Strousberg"), this was the second project of its kind in Romania (after the Bucharest-[Giurgiu](/wiki/Giurgiu "Giurgiu") rail link of 1869).[[16]](#cite_note-Giurescu,_p.155-16) The Pitești Town Hall was completed in 1886, and currently houses an art gallery.[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3) The [Argeș County](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County "Argeș County") Prefecture, designed by [Dimitrie Maimarolu](/wiki/Dimitrie_Maimarolu "Dimitrie Maimarolu"), was erected in 1898–1899 on the site previously occupied by an Orthodox [hermitage](/wiki/Hermitage_(religious_retreat) "Hermitage (religious retreat)"); it is the present-day site of the [County Museum of History and Natural Sciences](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County_Museum "Argeș County Museum").[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3) Both buildings are [eclectic](/wiki/Eclecticism "Eclecticism") in style, and feature [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") painted by [Iosif Materna](/w/index.php?title=Iosif_Materna&action=edit&redlink=1 "Iosif Materna (page does not exist)").[[3]](#cite_note-istoras-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/La_ville_de_Pitechti.jpg/250px-La_ville_de_Pitechti.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_ville_de_Pitechti.jpg)

Photo of Pitești c. 1893

Pitești experienced urbanization and economic development with several changes in landscape and a number of regional firsts. In 1868–1869, Pitești was the first city in Romania to have a recorded [Seventh-day Adventist](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") community, formed around [Michał Belina-Czechowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Belina-Czechowski "Michał Belina-Czechowski"), a [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") preacher and former Catholic priest who had returned from the United States (the [Seventh-day Adventist Church in Romania](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church_in_Romania "Seventh-day Adventist Church in Romania") was established only after 1918).[[17]](#cite_note-17) By 1906, Pitești was home to a thriving [cooperative bank](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking"), *Banca Populară Pitești*, which was also the first-ever financial institution in the Argeș County.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1907, factory manager Edmond Landauer performed the very first [Tayloristic experiments](/wiki/Scientific_management "Scientific management") in Romania, at Pitești Weaving Mill.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

From late autumn 1916 to 1918, during the [World War I battles](/wiki/Romanian_Campaign_(World_War_I) "Romanian Campaign (World War I)"), Pitești was occupied by the troops of the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"). The city was originally abandoned by the [Romanian Army](/wiki/Romanian_Army "Romanian Army") and taken by the German commander [August von Mackensen](/wiki/August_von_Mackensen "August von Mackensen") as the front stabilized on the [Olt River](/wiki/Olt_River "Olt River"), before Mackensen was able to occupy Bucharest and the entire southern Romania.[[20]](#cite_note-20) During the post-war existence of [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania"), Pitești became a regional cultural center, notably hosting the 1928–1929 series of the magazine *[Kalende](/w/index.php?title=Kalende&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kalende (page does not exist)")* (published in cooperation by literary critics [Vladimir Streinu](/wiki/Vladimir_Streinu "Vladimir Streinu"), [Șerban Cioculescu](/wiki/%C8%98erban_Cioculescu "Șerban Cioculescu"), [Pompiliu Constantinescu](/wiki/Pompiliu_Constantinescu "Pompiliu Constantinescu"), and [Tudor Șoimaru](/wiki/Tudor_%C8%98oimaru "Tudor Șoimaru")).[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### World War II and communism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World War II and communism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Pitesti_highrise_1970.jpg/250px-Pitesti_highrise_1970.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pitesti_highrise_1970.jpg)

Tower blocks in Pitești, photographed in 1970, shortly after their completion

Pitești was affected in various ways by World War II and its [successive regimes](/wiki/Romania_during_World_War_II "Romania during World War II"). After a [fascist](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") [National Legionary State](/wiki/National_Legionary_State "National Legionary State") was proclaimed by the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard") in late 1940, a bronze bust of former [premier](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_Romania "List of Prime Ministers of Romania") [Armand Călinescu](/wiki/Armand_C%C4%83linescu "Armand Călinescu") (whom the Guard had assassinated in September 1939), was chained and dragged through the city streets.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In December 1943, under the dictatorship of *[Conducător](/wiki/Conduc%C4%83tor "Conducător")* [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") (a Pitești native), it saw the final chapter in a chain of deportations of [Romani people](/wiki/Roma_minority_in_Romania "Roma minority in Romania") to [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria_(World_War_II) "Transnistria (World War II)") (*see [Holocaust in Romania](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Romania "Holocaust in Romania")*).[[23]](#cite_note-23) The city was sporadically bombed by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"): on July 4, 1944, it was struck by a section of the US [Fifteenth Air Force](/wiki/Fifteenth_Air_Force "Fifteenth Air Force") (*see [Bombing of Romania in World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Romania_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Romania in World War II")*).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In the 1950s, while serving as capital of [Regiunea Argeș](/wiki/Regiunea_Arge%C8%99 "Regiunea Argeș"), Pitești gained an ill notoriety, when the [communist authorities](/wiki/Communist_Romania "Communist Romania") used the local detention facility to subject political detainees to "[reeducation](/wiki/Reeducation_in_Communist_Romania "Reeducation in Communist Romania")", in which violence between inmates was encouraged to the point of being mandatory (*see [Pitești prison](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti_prison "Pitești prison")*). The experiment was carried out by the [Securitate](/wiki/Securitate "Securitate") secret police and overseen by [Alexandru Nicolschi](/wiki/Alexandru_Nicolschi "Alexandru Nicolschi"); its goal was to psychologically destroy the capacity for outside attachment and outside loyalty, creating the brainwashed *New Man* prototype of [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The program was canceled after some five years. At a trial held in 1953–1954, twenty-two inmate-participants were sentenced, with sixteen being condemned to death for their role in the experiment.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 1957, a new trial convicted certain members of the prison staff, who received light sentences; they were later pardoned.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In parallel, Pitești underwent numerous changes in landscape, including the completion of the [A1 freeway](/wiki/A1_(Romania) "A1 (Romania)"), the first road of its kind, during the 1960s, and the acceleration of [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") with a focus on the chemical and automotive industries. Around 1950, Pitești area accommodated [ELAS](/wiki/Greek_People%27s_Liberation_Army "Greek People's Liberation Army") refugees from the [Greek Civil War](/wiki/Greek_Civil_War "Greek Civil War") (some of the buildings erected for this purpose were later used to house resettled peasants).[[14]](#cite_note-mcraciun-14) *Florica* was [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") in 1948, and was later partly devastated by [Romanian Communist Party](/wiki/Romanian_Communist_Party "Romanian Communist Party") activists (for a while during the 1970s, it was a residence of the communist politician [Ion Dincă](/wiki/Ion_Dinc%C4%83 "Ion Dincă")).[[14]](#cite_note-mcraciun-14) The bust of [Ion Brătianu](/wiki/Ion_Br%C4%83tianu "Ion Brătianu"), standing in front of Saint Nicholas Church, was removed and melted, and the church itself was demolished in 1962.[[14]](#cite_note-mcraciun-14)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

The city is part of the [historical region](/wiki/Historical_regions_of_Romania "Historical regions of Romania") of Wallachia, situated in its north and in the westernmost part of its [Muntenian](/wiki/Muntenia "Muntenia") subregion. It lies on the right bank of the Argeș, where the river meets its tributary, [Râul Doamnei](/wiki/R%C3%A2ul_Doamnei "Râul Doamnei").

Pitești is situated 280 metres (918.64 [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)")) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"), on terraces formed by the Argeș, and belongs to the southernmost section of the [Getic Plateau](/w/index.php?title=Getic_Plateau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Getic Plateau (page does not exist)") (an area of foothills leading up to the [Southern Carpathians](/wiki/Southern_Carpathians "Southern Carpathians")).[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Plateau is at its narrowest in the Pitești area, where it only reaches 30 km (19 miles) in width, as opposed to the 70–80 km (43–50 miles) average.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The city has access to a piedmont plain, known as *Câmpia Piteștilor* ("Pitești Plain") and characterized by [water-meadows](/wiki/Water-meadow "Water-meadow").[[30]](#cite_note-30) To the west, it abuts the [Trivale Forest](/w/index.php?title=Trivale_Forest&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trivale Forest (page does not exist)"), which has been partly set up as a leisure park.

Pitești is adjacent to two reservoirs on the Argeș, in its Prundu area and in nearby [Bascov](/wiki/Bascov "Bascov") (the Budeasa Dam).[[31]](#cite_note-mmediubazinepilot-31) It is situated downstream from [Lake Vidraru](/wiki/Lake_Vidraru "Lake Vidraru") and upstream from the reservoir in Bălilești.[[31]](#cite_note-mmediubazinepilot-31)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), the city lies within the [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate#Mild/cool_summer_subtype "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*) category. Average annual temperatures range between 9 °C and 10 °C. The average January temperature is -2.4 °C, while the average July temperature is 20.8 °C.[[32]](#cite_note-geo-32)

As of [2021 census](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Romania "Demographic history of Romania") data, 141,275 inhabitants live within the city limits,[[37]](#cite_note-RPL2021-37) a decrease from the figure recorded at the 2011 census.

Romania's [2011 census](/wiki/2011_Romanian_census "2011 Romanian census") recorded a population of 155,383 for the city. Of the individuals for whom data were available, 99.1% were [ethnic Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") and 0.6% [Roma](/wiki/Roma_in_Romania "Roma in Romania").[[38]](#cite_note-INSSE-etnie-2011-38) In terms of religion, 98.4% were [Romanian Orthodox](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox "Romanian Orthodox"); 0.3% [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"); and 0.2% each [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal_Union_of_Romania "Pentecostal Union of Romania"), [Christian Evangelical](/wiki/Christian_Evangelical_Church_of_Romania "Christian Evangelical Church of Romania"), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist_Union_of_Romania "Baptist Union of Romania") and [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_Romania "Evangelical Church of Romania"). A further 0.2% were atheist or non-religious.[[39]](#cite_note-INSSE-religie-2011-39) A 2016 estimate placed the population at 177,485.[[32]](#cite_note-geo-32) The 1930 census found that 88.0% of residents were Romanians, 3.0% [Jews](/wiki/Romanian_Jews "Romanian Jews"), 2.5% [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), 2.2% [Roma](/wiki/Roma_in_Romania "Roma in Romania"), 1.4% [Germans](/wiki/Germans_of_Romania "Germans of Romania"), 0.6% [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks_of_Romania "Greeks of Romania") and 0.3% each [Russians](/wiki/Russians_in_Romania "Russians in Romania"), [Bulgarians](/wiki/Bulgarians_in_Romania "Bulgarians in Romania"), [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians_in_Romania "Armenians in Romania") and, grouped together, [Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Yugoslavs "Yugoslavs").[[40]](#cite_note-etnie1930-40)[[41]](#cite_note-etnie19302-41)

The population of Pitești grew between the 1830s and the 1990s, with the most sustained period of growth occurring after the 1950s, when industrial development created jobs and attracted residents from nearby settlements. The population peaked at an estimated 187,000 in 1997, then stagnated until 2001, and has gradually decreased since that time. The drop is particularly noticeable among the male population: this segment not only has a higher mortality and lower life expectancy, but also, due to de-industrialization, emigrated in search of work in significant numbers.[[42]](#cite_note-nedeleaPitești-42)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

Pitești is one of the most industrialized cities in Romania. It is the center of the automotive industry in the country: the [Automobile Dacia](/wiki/Automobile_Dacia "Automobile Dacia") automaker is situated in the nearby town of [Mioveni](/wiki/Mioveni "Mioveni"), and several other automobile parts manufacturers are located within its urban area ([Dräxlmaier Group](/wiki/Dr%C3%A4xlmaier_Group "Dräxlmaier Group"), [Lear Corporation](/wiki/Lear_(company) "Lear (company)") and [Valeo](/wiki/Valeo "Valeo")). The city also houses the [Arpechim](/wiki/Arpechim "Arpechim") oil refinery, part of the [Petrom](/wiki/Petrom "Petrom") group. The plant, established as a state-owned company during communism, has traditionally been the center of controversy over its [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") records. In 2007, the [Ministry of the Environment](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Environment_and_Sustainable_Development_(Romania) "Minister of the Environment and Sustainable Development (Romania)") withdrew Arpechim's permit, but Petrom contested the decision in court.[[43]](#cite_note-rolarpech-43) The plant is scheduled to gradually reduce its activity over a period of several years, pending eventual closure.[[43]](#cite_note-rolarpech-43)

The city is surrounded by hills rich in wineries and plum orchards. The latter give one of the finest Romanian *[țuicas](/wiki/%C8%9Auic%C4%83 "Țuică")*: *țuica de Pitești*. The Ștefănești winery, situated on the opposite bank of the Argeș River, is one of the best known in Romania.

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Theatre")]

Pitești is home to a County Theater; established in 1948, it was named in honor of playwright [Alexandru Davila](/wiki/Alexandru_Davila "Alexandru Davila") a decade later. Its branches include a [puppet theater](/wiki/Puppet_theater "Puppet theater") (created in 1949), the *Estrada* section for open-air performances (1958), and a [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") section (1970).[[44]](#cite_note-cjateatr-44) The Theater's Studio 125 was established in May 1975 by director [Liviu Ciulei](/wiki/Liviu_Ciulei "Liviu Ciulei").[[44]](#cite_note-cjateatr-44)

The first written record of a theatrical performance in the city dates to 1848, when [Constantin Halepliu](/w/index.php?title=Constantin_Halepliu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Constantin Halepliu (page does not exist)") set up a troupe. Following [that year's revolution](/wiki/Wallachian_Revolution_of_1848 "Wallachian Revolution of 1848"), several actors, Halepliu included, were arrested, and the theatre closed until 1856. A Communal Theatre was built in 1914–1916. Since 1948, the local acting ensemble has performed both in other Romanian cities and abroad, including in Poland, Serbia, Bulgaria, Italy and Spain.[[44]](#cite_note-cjateatr-44)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/RO_AG_-_Universitatea_din_Pitesti_%28fostul_Institutul_Pedagogic%29.jpg/250px-RO_AG_-_Universitatea_din_Pitesti_%28fostul_Institutul_Pedagogic%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:RO_AG_-_Universitatea_din_Pitesti_(fostul_Institutul_Pedagogic).jpg)

Faculty of Orthodox Theology at the [University of Pitești](/wiki/University_of_Pite%C8%99ti "University of Pitești")

The city houses two universities: the state-run [University of Pitești](/wiki/University_of_Pite%C8%99ti "University of Pitești") and the private [Constantin Brâncoveanu University](/wiki/Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncoveanu_University "Constantin Brâncoveanu University") (founded 1991, with branches in [Brăila](/wiki/Br%C4%83ila "Brăila") and [Râmnicu Vâlcea](/wiki/R%C3%A2mnicu_V%C3%A2lcea "Râmnicu Vâlcea")). There are 17 secondary education institutions, including two main high schools—the [Ion Brătianu National College](/wiki/Ion_Br%C4%83tianu_National_College_(Pite%C8%99ti) "Ion Brătianu National College (Pitești)") (founded 1866) and the [Zinca Golescu National College](/wiki/Zinca_Golescu_National_College "Zinca Golescu National College"). There are also 20 primary schools, 23 kindergartens and 10 nursery schools.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

A public library, named after intellectual figure [Dinicu Golescu](/wiki/Dinicu_Golescu "Dinicu Golescu"), was planned in 1869 by Paraschiva Stephu, a female member of the upper class, who drew up a will leaving 200 Austrian [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat") for the purpose of creating a library. The institution became operational in 1880, and a large part of the volumes were bequeathed by historian [George Ionescu-Gion](/w/index.php?title=George_Ionescu-Gion&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Ionescu-Gion (page does not exist)") immediately following his death in 1904. His donation included over a thousand books in Romanian, French and Italian.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-bibnat-47) The library moved into its current headquarters in the city center in 2003.[[47]](#cite_note-bibnat-47)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Festivals")]

Each year during springtime, Pitești is host to a festival and fair known as *Simfonia lalelelor* (the "Tulip Symphony"). Tulips were introduced locally in 1972–1973, when around 3,000 bulbs brought from [Arad](/wiki/Arad,_Romania "Arad, Romania") and [Oradea](/wiki/Oradea "Oradea") were planted in its central area, along with other flowers.[[48]](#cite_note-slalele-48) Pitești consequently acquired a reputation as a tulip-growing area, and the flower-themed festival was first organized by the local authorities in 1978.[[48]](#cite_note-slalele-48) Typically held in the cultural centre building (*Casa Cărții*), the festival also includes folk music performances, international scientific conferences, an art exhibit and youth sport competitions.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sport")]

The major [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in the city is [FC Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești"), which has generally played in [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), and has the [Nicolae Dobrin Stadium](/wiki/Nicolae_Dobrin_Stadium "Nicolae Dobrin Stadium") as its home ground.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) In addition, the city was home to a [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") football club, [Internațional Pitești](/wiki/FC_Interna%C8%9Bional_Curtea_de_Arge%C8%99 "FC Internațional Curtea de Argeș") (located on [Stadionul Ștrand](/wiki/Stadionul_%C8%98trand "Stadionul Ștrand")), and has a school which doubles as a junior team, Sporting Pitești.[[52]](#cite_note-sportingpit-52) Pitești hosts [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team [BCM U Pitești](/wiki/CSU_Pite%C8%99ti "CSU Pitești"), as well as a women's [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") team, Argeș Volei Pitești.

Pitești is home to an [Olympic size swimming pool](/wiki/Olympic_size_swimming_pool "Olympic size swimming pool"), the home ground for [CSM Pitești](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&redlink=1 "CSM Pitești (page does not exist)"), and a public outdoor swimming pool in the Tudor Vladimirescu area. Nearby [Bascov](/wiki/Bascov "Bascov") also has a public swimming place, on grounds adjacent to the Budeasa Dam. The national [canoe racing](/wiki/Canoe_racing "Canoe racing") also trains at the Budeasa Dam sports base, and the location is also used for recreational fishing. A [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") challenger tournament (*Turneul challenger feminin Pitești*) takes place each year, on grounds in Bascov.
